text: This video presents a technique for laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with extended lymphadenectomy. Previous reports proposed an oncologic benefit with a more radical lymphadenectomy in colon cancer surgery. However, while the technique was more widely adopted in left colon surgery, right-sided extended lymphadenectomy did not gain much popularity. A possible explanation is the more complex vascular anatomy of the right colon mesentery. Thus, initial focus has been on open methods.

The video demonstrates a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with extended lymphadenectomy, employing a medial-to-lateral, caudal-to-cranial approach. The surgical field shows the dissection of lymphatic tissue around the right colon mesentery in a forty-six-year-old male patient with cecal cancer.

The surgeon retracts the ileocolic vessels towards the right iliac fossa and pulls the transverse colon and omentum cranially. The right colon mesentery is opened posterior to the ileocolic vessels, and blunt dissection is used to separate the mesentery from the retroperitoneal fascia while preserving the visceral mesenteric envelope. The peritoneum over the superior mesenteric vein is opened from the ileocolic vessels towards the middle colic artery, exposing the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon retracts the ileocolic vessels anteriorly, extending the retro mesocolic dissection medially until the head of the pancreas is exposed. The extra length achieved on the ileocolic vessels is visible, and the ileocolic vein is exposed at its insertion to the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon performs a high division of the ileocolic vein and artery, continuing dissection towards the middle colic artery, maintaining a distance of two to three centimeters from the ileocolic vessel's origin. The gastrocolic trunk of Henle is exposed ventral to the pancreas head, with a deep mesenteric lymph node visibly present.

The surgeon dissects the middle colic artery and harvests an additional lymph node. The middle colic vein and the right branch of the middle colic artery are divided at their origin. The transverse colon and ileum are divided, and the colon is released from its lateral attachments. Upon completion of the resection, the mesocolon is fully dissected off the retroperitoneum, revealing the ureter, gonadal vessels, duodenum, and pancreas head.

The gastrocolic vein and middle colic artery are visible. The ileum and transverse colon are aligned in an isoperistaltic fashion, and an intracorporeal anastomosis is performed. The specimen is extracted through a five-centimeter transverse suprapubic incision. Forceps grasp the abscess of the mesenteric vessels, with a red line indicating the mesenteric transection line and a red circle marking additional lymph node retrieval. Blue lines show a complete, unviolated mesentery, confirming proper anatomical dissection.

The post-operative course was uneventful, and the patient was discharged home on the fifth day. On pathology, the tumor was classified as T-III-N-II-B. Thirty-four lymph nodes were extracted, and nine were involved. Both apical nodes of the iliocolic and middle colic arteries were not involved by cancer.
